one_line_takeaway: ICL security patterns (few-shot, CoT-based) can meaningfully but inconsistently reduce vulnerabilities in LLM-generated code — GitHub Copilot improved most (38% average) while Code Whisperer barely moved (6%) — and none of the four LLMs ever produced fully secure code, so LLM output still needs security review before deployment.
key_finding: In zero-shot generation all four LLMs produced insecure code — 111 CWEs total (~6 per program set); GitHub Copilot and Code Whisperer each produced 29 vulnerabilities. ICL security patterns reduced vulnerabilities but inconsistently: average zero-shot->few-shot reduction was GitHub Copilot 38%, ChatGPT 34%, Google Bard 23%, Code Whisperer 6%. Gains were highly problem-dependent and sometimes negative (e.g., Copilot cut Wildcard 75% but RESTful API worsened 17%; Bard improved MVC 57.14% but Wildcard rose 75%). CSRM stayed nonzero after ICL (ChatGPT ~20% DS&Algos, Bard ~14% RESTful API, Copilot ~8% SQLDD/MVC), and code smells persisted even after few-shot learning, so no method eliminated vulnerabilities.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a framework that uses In-Context Learning (ICL) "security patterns" — zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot examples built on Chain-of-Thought reasoning and drawn from OWASP ASVS / NIST SSDF principles — to steer LLM code generators toward more secure code, and then empirically measures the security of the generated code. The authors' own contributions (distinct from cited work) are: (1) the ICL security-pattern framework for injecting security knowledge during generation without fine-tuning; (2) a cross-cutting empirical study of four diverse code-generating LLMs across three languages, explicitly distinguishing Prompt-Driven Code Generators (PDCGs: ChatGPT-4, Google Bard) from fine-tuned Coding CoPilots (CCPs: GitHub Copilot, Amazon Code Whisperer); (3) a novel Code Security Risk Measure (CSRM = [Σ(CWEs + code smells) / Σ LOC] × 100); and (4) a promised release of a security-instructions dataset and a generated-code dataset for future secure-code research. Note: the widely cited "~40% of generated code is insecure" figure belongs to prior work [4,6,7], not to this paper's own measurements.
threat_model: The security concern is that LLM code generators, trained on public repositories containing insecure code, emit vulnerable code (CWEs such as improper input validation, SQL injection, path traversal, weak crypto/hashing, hardcoded credentials, cleartext transmission) that gets deployed into production and software supply chains, creating exploitable attack vectors for adversaries. The paper's proposed "defense" is embedding security knowledge at generation time via ICL security patterns rather than expensive fine-tuning; the evaluation measures residual vulnerabilities/code smells as the risk surface. This is not an adversarial attack on the model itself but a robustness/secure-code-generation threat model.
limitations: Small evaluation scope — only five programming problems across three languages and a handful of programs per LLM, so counts (single-digit CWEs per cell) are underpowered with no statistical significance testing. "Ground-truth" vulnerability counts depend entirely on the chosen SAST tools plus subjective manual code-smell review, and one SAST tool is itself LLM-based. Results are tied to specific 2024-era model versions (GPT-4, Google Bard/PaLM, Codex-based Copilot, Code Whisperer) and are not reproducible as models change. The CSRM metric is newly proposed and not independently validated. ICL sometimes increased vulnerabilities, and the paper acknowledges effectiveness is inconsistent and model/problem-specific. The promised datasets were not yet released. The writing is rough with several typos/inconsistent CWE labels (e.g., "CWE-811", "CWE-352 Information Exposure") that reduce confidence in exact CWE attributions.

datasets_used: Curated Data Structures & Algorithms problems in C++ from LeetCode and GeeksforGeeks (wildcard pattern matching, merging sorted linked lists, SQL de-duplication)
MVC design-pattern contact-management application in C# from C# Corner
60 experiments per problem set across the four LLMs and three shot conditions
Authors state they will release a security-instructions dataset and the generated-code dataset (not standard named benchmarks)
quantitative_results: Zero-shot: 111 total CWEs across the 4 LLMs, ~6 vulnerabilities per program set
GitHub Copilot and Code Whisperer each produced 29 zero-shot vulnerabilities
Zero-shot CWE counts (Wildcard/SortedList/SQLDD/MVC/RESTfulAPI): ChatGPT 5/4/5/5/7; Google Bard 4/4/4/5/6; GitHub Copilot 8/4/4/6/7; Code Whisperer 4/7/5/7/6
Average zero->few-shot vulnerability reduction: GitHub Copilot 38%, ChatGPT 34%, Google Bard 23%, Code Whisperer 6%

Per-category reductions: Copilot Wildcard 75%, MVC 33%, RESTful API -17%; Code Whisperer Sorted Linked List 29%, RESTful API 57%, Wildcard -57%; ChatGPT RESTful API 42.86%; Google Bard MVC 57.14%, Wildcard -75% (increase)
One-shot example: ChatGPT-4 wildcard vulnerabilities dropped from 5 (zero-shot) to 3 (one-shot) via input-validation/CWE-20 mitigation
Severity totals: ChatGPT 26 low / 21 medium; Google Bard 10 high / 21 low / 39 medium; GitHub Copilot 7 high / 26 medium / 30 low; Code Whisperer 33 high-severity
CSRM values: ChatGPT ~20% (DS&Algos), Google Bard ~14% (RESTful API), GitHub Copilot ~8% (SQLDD/MVC), Code Whisperer ~13% SQLDD / ~10% MVC
Linear-regression trend: PDCG code-quality impact dropped from 15% (DS&Algos) to 11% (RESTful API); CCPs showed a slight upward risk trend (>10%) on complex RESTful API tasks
SAST coverage: ~80% of vulnerabilities identified by Snyk Analyzer + AWS code scanner
baselines_compared: Zero-shot generation (no security context) as the baseline against one-shot and few-shot ICL security patterns
Cross-LLM comparison: ChatGPT-4 (GPT-4) and Google Bard (PaLM 540B) as PDCGs vs. GitHub Copilot (Codex 12B) and Amazon Code Whisperer as CCPs
No external competing secure-code-generation method is used as a baseline
